text: COEDANA, a parish in Anglesey, 2 m. SE of Llanerchymed. Pop. 275.
COEDCANLASS, a parish in Pembrokeshire, 6 m. SW of Haverford-West. Pop. 245.
COED-DHU-CHURCH, a parish in Glamorganshire, 21 m. E of Bridgend. Pop. 1,254.
COED-FRANK, a hamlet in the p. of Cadoxton, in Glamorganshire. Pop. in 1801, 391; in 1841, 1,126. The rapid increase of pop. has been occasioned by the formation of extensive copper-works here.
COEDGLASSION, a hamlet in the p. of Nantmel, in Radnorshire, 5 m. SE of Rhayadyr. Pop. 237.
COEDKERNEW, a parish in Monmouthshire, 4 m. SSW of Newport. Area 710 acres. Pop. 149.
COED-Y-CUMAR, a hamlet in the p. of Vainor, in Breconshire, 2 m. NE of Merthyr-Tydvil. Pop. in 1801, 889; in 1841, 1,905.
COEL, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Agra, 2 m. S by E of Alighur.
COELEBS, a village in Pittsylvania co., in Virginia, U. S., 174 m. SW by W of Richmond.

COEPANG, the principal Dutch settlement on the island of Timor, situated near the S extremity of a deep bay at the SW end of the island, in S lat. 10° 10' 11", and long. 9° 50' W of Swan river in Australia. The town is small and straggling, but neat and clean; and is occupied chiefly by the Europeans, Dutch half-eastes, and Chinese, the native pop. residing for the most part in huts scattered over the hills to the W of the town. The European inhabitants are about 12 in number, including the resident, one or two government-officers, and a few soldiers; the latter acting as non-commissioned officers to the Javanese troops, 40 in number, who garrison the little fort. There is not a single European merchant in C., the trade being conducted through the medium of the Chinese, who number about 300, and form the most | useful portion of the pop. The country-born descendants of the Dutch number about the same with the Chinese, and are remarkable only for an extreme indolence of disposition. A few of them are employed in subordinate situations under the government, but the greater portion subsist on the gains of their slaves; not a single individual among them possessing sufficient enterprise to cultivate a plantation, although the soil would amply repay their labour. A sugar plantation has lately been laid out, and planted by an English gentleman from the Mauritius, and should this succeed, some of the country-born inhabitants may possibly be induced to follow his example. It may appear surprising, that although this settlement has been in the possession of the Dutch upwards of two centuries, there are no wheeled carriages, nor a road on which they could travel; and this in a country in which horses are more numerous, in proportion to its size, than, perhaps, in any other part of the world. C. is not a favourable place for vessels to touch at for refreshments. Stock, with the exception of buffaloes, cannot be obtained in any considerable quantity without much delay, while both fruit and vegetables are scarce and dear; there are no port charges, but the duties on British goods are considerable, being 25 per cent. on calicoes, and 6 per cent. on all other, except muskets and gunpowder, the duties on these being a dollar each for the former, and a dollar a cask for the latter. The duty, however, is only demanded for the goods that are actually sold or intended to be left for sale; our ships, therefore, carry on little trade with C. itself, but proceed to some of the smaller ports along the coast, where there are no duties, and where the articles they require are in abundance. The rajahs of the W portion of Timor receive their appointment from the resident at C.
COESFELD, a town of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the gov. and 18 m. W of Munster. Pop. 3,400.
COESMES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Rhetiers. Pop. 1,537.
COETBO, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. and com. of Guer. Pop. 104. There is an agricultural school here.
COETIVI, an island 6° W of Mauritius, in S lat. 7° 15', E long. 56° 23'. It is about 9 m. in circumf., and affords maize, turtle, and cocoa-nut oil. Pop. 100.
COETMEN, a town of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, com. of Lezardrieux, on the Trieux, near its embouchure, which here forms a fine port.

COEUVE, or Kuef, a parish and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, bailiwick of Porentruy. Pop. 578, Catholic. CŒUVRES, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Vic-sur-Aisne, 8 m. WSW of Soissons. Pop. 693.

COEVORDEN, a town of Holland, in the prov. of Drenthe, on the Drosten-diep branch of the Vecht, 21 m. E by S of Meppel. Pop. 1,600. It is surrounded by low marshy ground; and is strongly fortified, forming the key to Groningen, Overyssel, and Friesland. It has the form of a regular pentagon, with high and thick ramparts, 7 large bastions, 7 halfmoons, and 7 ravelins. It is farther strengthened by a fort surrounded with 5 bastions.—In 1672, it was besieged by Bernard von Galen, the warlike bishop of Munster, and betrayed by the governor; but was shortly after retaken by the Dutch. In 1795, it surrendered to the French, after they were masters of Holland.
COEX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Saint-Gilles-sur-Vie. Pop. 1,050. COG
COEYMANS, a township in Albany co., in the state of New York, U. S., 13 m. S of Albany. Pop. 3,107.
COFFEE, a county in the central part of the state of Tennessee, U.S., intersected by the head-branches of Duck river. Area 170 sq. m. Pop. 8,184. The cap. is Manchester.
COFFEE-CREEK, a village in Porter co., in the state of Iowa, U. S., 148 m. NNW of Indianapolis.
COFFEETON, a village in Wabash co., in the state of Illinois, U. S., 170 m. SE of Springfield.

COFFEEVILLE, a village in Yallabusha co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., 130 m. N by E of Jackson on Turkey creek, a branch of the Yazoo.—Also a v. in Clark co., in the state of Alabama, on the E bank of the Tombigbee river.
COFFIN HARBOUR, an anchorage on the E side of New Island, in the Falkland group, in S lat. 51° 43′ 10″, W long. 61° 17′ 22″.

COFFIN-ISLAND, one of the Magdalen group, in the entrance of the gulf of St. Lawrence. It extends 4 m. W of the Old Harry. At its SW end is the narrow entrance of Grand Entry harbour, which has water enough within for large vessels. There is a revolving light on the S point of this island, in N lat. 44° 1′ 50", W long. 64° 40′ 49".

COFFINSWELL, a parish of Devonshire, 4 m. E by S of Newton. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 215. COFLAN-KOU [Morier], or KOFLAN-KOUS [Monteith], a mountain-range of Persia, on the SE frontiers of Azerdbijan, through which the Kizil-Ouzan river forces its way, forming a defile hardly practicable for cattle. Morier says he was just one hour in gaining the greatest height of the C. from the bank of the Kizil-Ouzan, and half-an-hour in descending into the plain on the opposite side.
COFRENTES, or Cofruentes, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Valencia, partido of Jarafuel, at the confluence of the Cabriel and the Xucar. Pop. 1,758
COGAN, a parish of Glamorganshire, 41 m. SSW of Cardiff. Pop. 28.
COGENHOE, or COOKNOE, a parish of Northamptonshire, 5½ m. E of Northampton, S of the Nen. Area 960 acres. Pop. 322.

COGGESHALL (GREAT), a parish and market town of Essex, 6 m. ESE of Braintree. Area 2,770 acres. Pop. 3,408. The town is situated on the N side of the Blackwater; and has some velvet and silk manufactures.
COGGESHALL (LITTLE), a parish of Essex, } m. S of Great Coggeshall, and on the opposite bank of the Blackwater. Area 830 acres. Pop. 443.
COGGS, or Cogges, a parish of Oxfordshire, about a mile E of Witney. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 757.

COGHINAS, or Coguinas, a river of the island of Sardinia,—the second largest in the island,—in the div. of Sassari, which takes its rise in Monte Acuto, 14 m. SSE of Ozieri, the name of which town it bears until its confluence with the Salema, whence it is known as the Rio-Grande until its entrance into the district of Coghinas. It falls into the Mediterranean between Castel-Sardo and Isola-Rossa. after a total course in a generally NNW direction of about 54 m.
COGLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Saint-Brice-en-Cogles, on the Tronson. Pop. 1,403.

COGNAC, an arrondissement, canton, and town of France, in the dep. of La Charente. - The arrond. comprises 4 cant., viz.: Chateauneuf-sur-Charente, C., Jarnac, and Segonzac. Area 74,111 hectares. Pop. in 1841, 52,301.—The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1841, 15,885.—The com. and town are situated on the l. bank of the Charente, 18 m. NNW of Barbezieux. It contains numerous brandy distilleries, and is the staple place for the brandies produced in the surrounding districts, amounting to about 6,000 tiercons or butts annually. Pop. in 1789, 3,332; in 1821, 3,009; in 1841, 4,118.—Also a com. in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Saint-Laurentsur-Gorre. Pop. 1,800.
COGNE, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. and 8 m. S of Aosta, on a stream of the same name. Pop. 1,500. The valley of C. contains mines of iron, copper, and manganese.
COGNI. See KONIEH.
COGNIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Vinay. Pop. 1,146.
COGNITZ. See KONITZA.
GOGNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. and 3 m. W of Villefranche. Pop. 1,008.
COGOLIN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Var, cant. of Grimaud. Pop. 1,289.
COGOLLOR, a village of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. ENE of Guadalaxara.
COGOLLOS, a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 10 m. S of Burgos, partido of Lerma.
COGOLLOS-DE-GUADIX, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 8 m. E of Grenada, at the foot of the Sierra-Nevada.
COGSHALL, a township in the p. of Great Budsworth, in Cheshire. 31 m. NNW of Northwich. Area 520 acres. Pop. 108.
COGULLUDO, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 27 m. N of Guadalaxara, on an affluent of the Henares.

COHAHUILA, or COAHUILA, a prov. of Mexico, bounded on the N by the Rio-Bravo-del-Norte, which separates it from Texas; on the E by the same river, and the states of Tamaulipas, and Nuevo Leon; on the S by Zacatecas; and on the W by Durango and Chihuahua. It lies between the parallels of 24° 15′ and 30° N, and the meridians of 100° and 104°. Its length from N to S, measured along the meridian of 102°, is 390 m. Its greatest breadth is 270 m. By the Mexican constitution of 1824, the states of C. and Texas were returned as provisionally one, and their joint area was estimated at 136,500 sq. m., with a pop. of only 46,000. The extent of the present state of C. is about 40,000 sq. m., and the pop. in 1839 was estimated at 82,000. Towards the S the face of the country is level; the N districts have a hilly and broken surface. The W portion is a mountainous desert tract, called the Bolson-de-Mapimi, uninhabited save by bands of roving Indians. A considerable quantity of grain is raised in this state, and good wine is manufactured; but the rearing of cattle and horses forms the principal employment of its pop. The cap. is Saltillo. The other towns are Monclova, Santa-Rosa, Castanuela, and Parras.
COHASSET, a township of Norfolk co., in the state of Massachussets, U. S., 20 m. SE of Boston. The coast is rocky and dangerous. Pop. in 1840, 1,471.

COHAT, a town and fort of Afghanistan, in the prov. and 28 m. SSE of Peshawur, on the S side of the Kyber mountains, midway between Peshawur and Kalabagh. The Sikhs held possession of this place, though isolated from their other territories, and surrounded by Afreedies, an intractable Afghan tribe, who will probably afford the British holders of C. no trifling occupation for some years to come.

COHOES, a village of Watervliet township, Albany co., in the state of New York, 8 m. N of Albany, on the SW bank of the Mohawk river, 2 m. above its mouth, a little below Cohoes falls, and near the junction of the Erie and Champlain canals. Pop. in 1840, about 2,000. The river at C. falls is from 300 to 400 ft. in breadth, and has a perpendicular fall of 70 ft. COI
COIFFY-LE-HAUT, or COIFFY-LE-CHATEAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, cant. of Bourbonne. Pop. 1,072.
COIGACH (THE AIRD OF), a district of Cromarty, situated within the p. of Loch-Broom, Rossshire. Pop. in 1834, 1,975.
COILCONDAH, a district and town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 53 m. SW of Hydrabad. The district, which is mountainous, is bounded on the S by the Kistna.

COILLE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, 32 m. NNW of Durbungah, and 65 m. NNE of Patna. COIMBATORE, COIMBATOOR, OF KOYAMATURA, a small province of Hindostan, situated on the declivity of the Eastern Ghauts, in about the 11th parallel of N lat. It is bounded on the N by Mysore; on the E by the districts of Salem and Trichinopoly; on the S by Dindigul; and on the W by Malabar. Its length from N to S is 50 m.; the breadth from E to W about 45 m. Towards the S the level is not more than 400 or 500 ft. above the sea, but it gradually rises towards the N; and even in what is considered the low country, the level rises to 900 ft. above the sea. In about N lat. 11° 35½, the Kumbetarine hill is reckoned to be 5,548 ft. above sea-level. [Lambton.] Some summits of the Neilgherry mountains, in the NW part of the prov. which unite the E and W Ghauts are still higher: one of the peaks called Murchute-Bet has an alt. of 8,800 ft. above sea-level. The soil is generally dry, and strongly impregnated with salts and nitrates; but in the S there is some low and marshy ground, amounting to about 3 per cent. of the whole. The climate is considered healthy, and in particular the Neilgherry mountains are resorted to by Europeans resident in India for the recovery of their health. In these hills the mean temp. in April and May is 65°. During the cold season the therm. sometimes sinks to the freezing point, when the air is peculiarly clear and elastic. Heavy dews are experienced in January and February.—C. is watered by the Bhavani, Amaravati, and Cavery. The Bhavani crosses the N districts, and joins the Cavery at Bhavani-Kudal, 58 m. NE from the town of Coimbatore, in N lat. 11° 26'; and the Amaravati, or Karur, joins the Cavery about 10 m. below the town of Karur. The channels of these rivers are filled by both monsoons; by the SW in June, July, and August; and by the NE in October, November, and December.—Sheep of two different kinds are extensively reared in this prov. One is short-bodied, with a short small tail; the other is of a thinner and longer make. The black bear occurs in the mountains.—The extent of land under cultivation in 1814-15, was 960,000 acres; in 1825-26, it had increased to 1,451,439 acres; and in 1836-7, it amounted to 3,289,327 acres. Its value depends principally upon the facility with which it can be irrigated. Rice is the grain chiefly cultivated; but sugar and tobacco are produced; and experiments in the culture of cotton have been pursued here, under the superintendence of Dr. Wight, since 1842. It appears that whenever the land has been carefully prepared for the seed, the crop of New Orleans cotton is abundant; yet, at the end of ten years' experience, Dr. Wight is of opinion, that in order to induce the natives to apply themselves to the cultivation of cotton a remission of 50 per cent. on the assessment of all land so employed will be found necessary. Up to the former period the land-revenue of the prov. was collected under the village-system; but a permanent assessment being then made, and the ryots being confirmed in the possession of their lands, the improvement here noticed began. In 1814-15 the land revenue was 18,64,391 rupees; in 1825-26 it had advanced to 23,79,633 rupees; being an annual increase of £51,524; in 1836-7 it amounted to 20,84,913 rupees. In 1814-15 the government assessment was considered to be equal to one-third of the gross produce of the soil, and in 1825-26 it did not exceed one-fifth; the price of land during that time was doubled. The only manufacture is that of cotton cloth. The pop. of the prov. in 1836-7 was 807,964; in 1823 it was stated to be 638,199; but in this latter number children under 5 years of age were not included. The natives are almost exclusively Hindus.

COIMBATORE, the capital of the above prov., situated on an elevated position, on an affluent of the Cavery, in N lat. 10° 52', and E long 77° 5', 90 m. SSE of Mysore, is a well-built town, containing about 2,000 houses, being little more than half the number which it contained under the government of Hyder Ali. His son Tippu sometimes resided at C., where he built a mosque. About 2 m. from the town, at a place called Peruru, is a celebrated temple dedicated to Iswara, and called Mail-Chitumbra, to distinguish it from another Chitumbra near Pondicherry. This building exhibits a profusion of Hindu ornaments, carved in granite; but is destitute of elegance. It was plundered of its gold and jewels by Tippu, who excepted it, however, from the general order which he issued for the destruction of all idolatrous buildings; and although its splendour was then destroyed, the Brahminical worship has always been maintained in it.—C. was taken by the English in 1783, but was restored in the following year. It was again taken in 1790, and retaken by Tippu's general, but was transferred with the prov. to the British government in 1799, and has since remained in their possession. It was greatly depopulated by an epidemic in 1809-11. The travelling distance from Seringapatam is 122 m., from Madras 306 m. COIMBRA [French, Coimbre], a comarca and city of Portugal, in the prov. of Beira, 120 m. NNE of Lisbon, in N lat. 40° 12½, at the mouth of the Ceira, an affluent of the Mondego, on the high road from Lisbon to Oporto, and at an alt. of 301 ft. above sea-level. The situation of C. is extremely delightful. The surrounding country, though mountainous, is fertile and highly cultivated. The Portuguese landscape is generally deficient in trees; but here the mountains, covered with groves of pines and German oaks, embosom valleys of exquisite beauty, whose elegant quintas, and neat summer-houses, are finely contrasted with the stately solemnity of the monastic buildings; and where brooks, flowing through luxuriant gardens, or gleaming amidst the dark foliage of olive-trees, orange-trees, and the beautiful Portuguese cypress (Cupressus Lusitanica), originally brought from Goa to Busaco, give freshness and animation to the scene. The olive abounds in the neighbourhood of C., and maize is cultivated in the district to a great extent. From one of these valleys, through which the Mondego rolls his broad and winding stream, C. rises, built partly on the plain contiguous to the river, partly along the W declivity of a steep rocky hill, bursting at once on the view of the traveller, in all the magnificence of spires, churches, and monasteries. Opposite to C., in the romantic valley of the Mondego, is the Quinta das Lagrimas, or 'garden of tears,'—a spot "over which fancy delights to hover" as the scene where the young and beautiful Inez de Castro was slain while awaiting her princely husband. The view is closed by the lofty mountains of Lousao on one side; and on the NE by the mountain-ridge of Busaco, whose solitary summit is adorned by a celebrated monastery of Carmelites. On entering the town, however, the feelings and expectations which its situation and distant appearance had excited, are suddenly exchanged for COI disappointment and disgust. C. seems to have been built in meditated defiance of all the rules of convenience and taste. Its streets are narrow, crooked, full of angles, ill-paved, and dirty; and frequently so steep that they cannot be climbed without difficulty and toil. The only broad street is in that small part of the town which is situated on the plain; but even that street, being deemed unhealthy in summer, is the residence only of the poorer class of citizens. Here are no open spaces or promenades, so necessary to the health and comfort of the inhabitants in a warm climate. The only resort of the citizens in their pleasure-walks is to the great quinta of the Augustines. C. is a place of antiquity, and had sustained many sieges before Count Henriquez took possession of Portugal. The ancient walls and towers still remain, and form the only defence of the city. Since the year 1419, it has been in a state of decline. Even yet, however, it contains more than 3,000 houses, with a pop. of 18,000. It is divided into 9 parishes; and has no less than 7 monasteries, one of which, that of Santa Cruz, is the second in point of wealth in the kingdom. C. is the see of a bishop, who is always Count of Arganuil; and it has its corregidor, its provedor, and juiz-de-fora, or court of justice. The chief manufactures consist of pottery, and this branch of art has been carried to a high degree of perfection here. Woollen and linen cloths are also manufactured in C., and the kingdom is supplied with horn-combs and wooden tooth-picks from this locality. By far the most important object at C. is the university, founded at Lisbon by Don Deniz in 1291, but transferred hither after a period of 16 years. It was again removed to Lisbon, but in 1537 was a second time transferred by John to C., where it has since remained. This university is under the government of a council, consisting of a rector nominated by the sovereign, the deans of the several faculties, 2 sindicoes or fiscals, and a secretary. The office of rector—which is never conferred on a member of the university—is triennial; and is generally held by a priest, who, after the term of his rectorship has expired, is either re-elected or promoted to a bishop's see. Superior to the rector is the reformator; but both these offices are frequently united in one person. The pecuniary transactions of this rich establishment are managed by a junta de fazenda, or auxiliary council-of-finance, consisting of a professor of theology, a professor of law, the professor of calculation, with a treasurer and clerk. The prior and chief of the Augustine canons regular at C. is ex-officio chancellor of the university. He is the principal superintendant in matters of learning, conferring the degrees at all promotions, and presiding at the examinations of students. In this university the degrees are very numerous; and, if the division of labour be sufficient to insure excellence, the professors of C. ought to be particularly eminent, for there are 33 professors in ordinary, and 22 assistant-professors, attached to the university. The field of study is here divided into 6 departments, - theology, canon law, jurisprudence, medicine, mathematics, and philosophy. The session commences in October, and closes in May, after which the public examinations continue till the month of July. These examinations are carried on in the Portuguese language, and are understood to be severe. The lectures are likewise delivered in Portuguese, and in all respects resemble those delivered in other universities, except that the students pay nothing for hearing them. Before a student in theology, jurisprudence, or medicine, can hold an office, or exercise his profession, he must have studied at C. a certain number of years, attended certain lectures, and gone through his annual examinations. The term prescribed for theology is five years. The number of | students at C. is considerable. Some Portuguese writers make their number amount to 2,000. In 1798 they were 800; in 1804, 1,431; in 1817, 1,400. The students are distinguished by a particular dress, but live, as with us, in private houses, and not within the walls of the university. The museum of the university contains few remarkable specimens; but the collection of philosophical instruments is excellent, containing many of English manufacture. In mechanics also it is very rich; and the chemical laboratory is capacious and well furnished. The number of volumes in the library is about 60,000. The observatory, which stands in an excellent situation in the upper end of the town, is convenient and neatly arranged, with a good collection of London-made instruments. The botanic garden, though not large, is admirably regulated, forming almost an exact counterpart to the garden of Paris. Besides many exotics, it contains a considerable number of plants indigenous in Portugal. The university is well provided with funds. Some commanderies of the order of Christ are attached to the office of senior lecturer in mathematics and natural philosophy, while every cathedral throughout the kingdom is obliged to attach a certain number of its prebendal stalls to the different faculties. The expenses of tuition are defrayed by the revenues of the university; and the students only pay a few trifling fees.—C. was the native place of Diego de Paiva d'Andrada and Thomas Correa, both celebrated writers; also of the early poet Francisco Saa de Miranda.
COIMBRA (Nova), a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Mato-Grosso, on the r. bank of the Paraguay, and the E declivity of the Serra-das-Grutas-do-Inferno, in S lat. 19° 55'.

COIN, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 22 m. WSW of Malaga, at the confluence of the Beringuel with the Rio-Seco. Pop. 6,000. It is situated some way up the N acclivity of a high wooded hill, and commands a splendid view of the valley of the Guadaljorce. The houses are good, and the streets well-paved.
COINA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, 8 m. NNW of Setuval.
COINCY-L'ABBAYE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Fere-en-Tardenois. Pop. 1,078.
COIRE. See CHUR.
COISE, a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Savoy, 12 m. SE of Chambery. Pop. 1,450.
COITSVILLE, a township in Trumbull co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 182 m. NE of Columbus, on the Beaver river. Pop. 1,005.
COJANI, a town of Turkey, in Macedonia, in the sanjak of Monastir, 12 m. N by W of Servia, on the road to Chatista. Pop. 2,500.

COJUTEPEKE, a lake of Guatemala, in the district of San Salvador. It is about 12 m. in length, and on an average 5 m. broad, and is surrounded on all sides by majestic and precipitous mountains. In smooth weather it has no peculiar appearance; but after a brisk wind it assumes a dark green colour, and the fish flock to the shores in such numbers that they are taken in vast quantities with nets, or even by the hand. This singular phenomenon is called by the natives la cosecha de pescados, or 'the fish-harvest.'

COKALAHISKIT, a river of Oregon, which rises in the Rocky mountains, and, after a NW course of about 200 m., enters Clarke's river, a branch of the Columbia, in N lat. 46° 45′, W long. 114° 20′. It is deep and rapid, and about 60 yards wide, with banks which, though not high, are sufficiently bold to prevent the waters from overflowing. The mountains, in which it has its source, form the dividing ridge between the is extremely active, consists in grain, oil, wine, fish, COL waters that run into the Atlantic and those that run into the Pacific; and it approaches within 60 m. of Dearborn's creek, which falls into the Missouri.
COKE (Point), the NW point of entrance into Holkham-bay, on the W coast of North America, in N lat. 57° 43'.

COKER (East and West), two parishes in Somersetshire, the former 2½ m., the latter 3 m. SW of Yeovil. Area of East C. 2,140 acres. Pop. 1,334. Area of West C. 1,100 acres. Pop. 1,046.—The great navigator Dampier was a native of East Coker.
COKLAY. See KOKELAY.

COL, a general designation given to those mountains in the Alps and the Apennines over which a pass leads. The name is frequently applied both to the mountain itself and to the pass; but should be understood as designating rather the pass, than the mountain in a depression of which or across a lower shoulder of which the road is conducted. In the Pyrenees the term port is synonymous with col.
COLABA. See BOMBAY.

COLAGAL, or COLLEGAL PETTAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 80 m. NNE of Coimbatur, and 33 m. SE of Seringapatam. It contained in 1820 2 Hindu temples, and about 600 dwellings. Two streets consisted of tiled, white-washed, and uniformly built houses. The inhabitants are chiefly weavers, and the trade of the place is considerable.

COLAIR, a peel or lake of Hindostan, in the prov. of the Southern Circars, 3 m. E of Ellore, between the Godaveri and Kistna, from the overflowings of which rivers it obtains the greater portion of its waters. Its breadth varies from 7 to 12 m., and its extreme length is about 22 m. It contains upwards of a dozen small islands, which are rendered highly productive by the periodical inundations of the lake. Its chief outlet is the Ooputnair.
COLAN, a parish of Cornwall, 31 m. SW of St. Columb-Major. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 217.
COLAN, a town of Peru, in the intendancy of Truxillo, prov. of Piura, at the entrance of the river of the same name into the Pacific. See CATAMAYU.
COLANCELLE, a small river of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, which issues from a large pond named Gouffier, and, after a course of about 5 m., falls into the Yonne. It becomes dry in summer.
COLAPUR. See KOLAPUR.

COLAR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, 40 m. NE of Bangalore, 110 m. NE of Seringapatam. It is enclosed by a mud wall, and defended by a fortress built of stone; and contains a mosque, a Mussulman college, the tomb of Feth-Mahommed, and about 700 houses. In possesses considerable manufactories of cotton. In the environs are numerous gardens in a high state of cultivation. C. was taken by the English in 1768, but was soon after regained by Hyder-Ali.

COLASTRY, a zemindary of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, extending W from the Nellore district, and to the N of the Pennar river. It possesses little that is attractive in its external aspect, but contains extensive copper-mines, the principal of which lie about 50 m. NW of Nellore.
COLATOOR, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, in the prov. and 20 m. S of Trichinopoly, on the E bank of a small lake.

COLBERG, or Kolberg, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Pomerania, gov. and 25 m. W of Coslin, circle of Fürstenthum, and 75 m. NE of Stettin, on the Persante, 13 m. above its entrance into the Baltic. Pop. 7,610. Its principal buildings are the cathedral, the town-house, the citadel, and the agneduct by which the town is supplied with water. It contains also 4 churches, a college, a bank, a customhouse, a house-of-correction, &c. The trade, which ~ potash, glass, iron, woollen and linen fabrics, &c. The harbour is small, but good.
COLBERT, a village of Lowndes co., in the state of Mississippi, U. S., 155 m. NE of Jackson, on the W side of Tombigbee.

COLBORNE, a district of Upper Canada to the N of Newcastle district, and comprising the townships of Asphodel, Belmont, Burleigh, Bexley, Dummer, Douro, Ennismore, Emily, Eldon, Fenelon, Harvey, Methuen, Mariposa, Otonabee, Ops, Smith, Somerville, Verulam, and North Monaghan. Pop. in 1842, 13,706. Acres under cultivation in 1844, 58,492. Number of schools, 72.—Also a township of Upper Canada, in the Huron district, bounded by Lake Huron on the W, and Maitland river on the S. Area 35,460 acres. Pop. 505.—Also a v. in the townships of Cramahe and Murray, in Upper Canada, 16 m. E of Coburg. Pop. 400.
COLBOURN, or Colborn, a township in the p. of Catterick, N. R. of Yorkshire, 22 m. ESE of Richmond. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 142.

COLBY, a parish of Norfolk, 31 m. NNE of Aysham, W of the Bure. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 346. -Also a township in the p. of St. Lawrence, Appleby, Westmoreland, 13 m. WNW of Appleby, S of the Eden. Pop. 156.—Also a village in the p. of Arbory, Isle of Man. Pop. 149.
COLCABAMBA, a town of Peru, in the intendancy of Ayacucho, district of Tayacaxa, 50 m. NW of Huamanga.

COLCHAGUA, a province of the republic of Chili; bounded on the N by Santiago, from which it is separated by the Cachapool river; on the E by the Andes; on the S by the Teno, which divides it from the prov. of Maule; and on the W by the Pacific. It is about 150 m. in length, and from 30 to 35 m. in breadth. Its climate is temperate, and soil fertile. It is watered by numerous small rivers, of which the principal are the Rio Claro, Tinguiririca, Chimbarongo, Teno, and Nilahue, with several lakes, of which Taguatague and Caguil are the largest. Immense forests cover a considerable portion of its S districts. The cap. is San Fernando, in S lat 34° 18', which is inhabited by about 1,500 families. Talca is also a place of some importance in this prov.

COLCHESTER, a parliamentary borough and market-town, in the co. of Essex; 514 m. NE by E of London by railroad, and 22 m. NE of Chelmsford, on the road from Harwich by Chelmsford to London; and on the line of the Eastern Counties railway from London to Bury. Area of borough and liberty, 11,770 acres. Pop. in 1801, 11,620; in 1831, 16,167; in 1841, 17,790. C. is comprised within the diocese of London, and gives title to one of the five archdeaconries attached to that see. The archdeaconry comprehends the deaneries of Colchester, Lexden, Tendring, Witham, Samford, and Newport. Besides the parishes of Lexden, Milend, Greenstead, and Berechurch, in the liberties of the borough, but without the town, there are 12 parishes within the limits of C.—The principal part of the town occupies the summit, and N, and E sides of a fine eminence nearly in the centre of the borough, rising gradually from the river Colne, which is navigable for vessels of about 150 tons burthen, to the Hythe, a long suburban street, running along the river-side, and forming the port or harbour of C. Vessels of greater tonnage, principally colliers, must discharge their cargoes at Wivenhoe, about 3 m. lower down the river. The merchandise, however, is brought in smaller craft to the Hythe, where are situated most of the warehouses, the coal, timber, and bonded yards, and the custom-house. The Colne encircles and intersects part of the town, on the N and NE sides, and is crossed by several bridges. The town was formerly 614 surrounded by walls, which are still in many places entire. To the N of the High-street, on an elevated spot, and commanding a fine view of the winding valley to the N and E, stands the castle. Its outer walls are nearly perfect; and their vast thickness and solidity evince the importance which, in early ages, was attached to this situation. The churches, with the ruins of St. John's abbey, St. Botolph's priory, and the Moot-hall, constitute the chief of the other ancient or public buildings. Many of the houses have a pervading air of venerable antiquity about them, which never fails to create an interest. The town is well supplied with water from springs and wells, as well as from water-works established in 1808, and kept in operation by a steam engine.-The trade of C., except its shipping concerns, chiefly consists, like that of most inland towns, in the supply of the agricultural classes with manufactures, &c., in return for agricultural produce. Formerly, however, this town was remarkable for its successful cultivation of a particular branch of woollen manufacture denominated 'the bay and say,' or baize and serge trade, originally introduced by the expatriated Flemings, who settled here in the reign of Elizabeth. But prior even to the statute of Edward III., -which prohibits the exportation of wool, and which has been followed by similar acts, down to the 7th of George II.,-it appears that this staple of the nation was made into cloth here; and for some generations after the reign of Elizabeth, this town had nearly a monopoly of the baize and serge manufacture. The war with Spain, in the reign of Anne, was the great occasion of its downfall. Individuals, however, continued it with some spirit for about half a century afterwards; but for the last 40 years it may be said to have been extinct. The first invention in the process of manufacturing cotton, 'the fly-shuttle,' was invented in 1738, by John Kay, then residing here. It was brought into general use about 1760, and was found greatly to abridge the time and exertion required in weaving. Robert, the son of John, added to this 'the drop-box.' But so far were the inventors from profiting by these discoveries, that Mr. Kay could only escape the persecution and danger to which, in consequence of his invention for abridging manual labour, he became exposed, by flying to Paris. The cotton manufacture was never established here, however; and that of woollen was superseded by the silk manufacture, which was introduced about 50 or 60 years ago. There were about 160 silk looms here at one time; but this branch of manufacture has also gradually declined. There are now not above from 40 to 60 looms in the town; but a large silk factory was erected a few years ago in the parish of St. Martin. Umbrella and parasol silk, and handkerchiefs, were the chief manufacture. The removal of the great military establishments which existed here during the last war has also injuriously affected the trade of C. The celebrated oyster fisheries of C. likewise give profitable exployment to many, and have always formed a comparatively valuable part of the trade of this town. The spat, or brood whence the oysters are chiefly obtained, is taken principally in the river Blackwater, after which it is deposited on beds in the neighbourhood of C. and Malden. Richard I. granted the burgesses the fishery of the river Colne, from the North Bridge as far as Westnesse, which grant was amply confirmed by subsequent charters. For the better preservation of this privilege, courts of admiralty, or conservancy, have been customarily held on Colne water. The river is yearly shut against fishing till 22d July. C. is a bonding port. There are bonded warehouses for wine, spirits, corn, and wood goods; but the foreign trade is trifling; it consists principally in wines from Spain, oil-cakes from Holland, and timber from the Baltic. The port, however, carries on a general coasting-trade with London, principally in colonial and manufactured goods; and with the northern counties of England in coals, tiles, &c. The exports are corn and malt. Upwards of fifty vessels of different sizes and denominations belong to C.; but a great many small craft, belonging to Wivenhoe, Rowhedge, Brightlingsea, and other small places, lower down on the Colne, all "hail" from this port, which makes the shipping of C. appear more numerous than it really is. The present flow of the tide at the Hythe is only 6 ft. 8 in.; but the channel might be improved so as to admit of vessels of 18 ft. draught reaching the Hythe at ordinary spring-tides. Fairs are held in July, principally for horses and cattle; and in October for horses, cattle, and miscellaneous merchandise. An attempt is now making to re-establish an annual wool mart in C .- The parliamentary and municipal boundaries of this borough are co-extensive. The borough comprehends 12 town, and 4 outlying, parishes. The liberties form a compact district all in the co. of Essex. Under the municipal reform act, the borough is divided into 3 wards, and is governed by 6 aldermen and 18 councillors. With some intermission, 2 members have been sent, by this borough, to parliament, since 1296; and under the reform act, the privilege of returning two members has been continued. The number of electors registered in 1837, was 1,176; in 1847, 1,257. NE of Eccleshall, Staffordshire. Area 1,470 acres. C. is one of the polling-places for the N division of Essex. History.] The borough of C. was designated, in the time of Edward IV., as "one of the ancientest burghs in the kingdom of England." At the time of the Norman survey it appears to have been a flourishing town. In 1665, the great plague which ravaged London extended its baneful influences to C., and swept away nearly 5,000 of its inhabitants. During the late war, C. was the site of an extensive military establishment. The barracks, except a few officers' houses, were pulled down at the close of the war; but a small military force has ever since been continued at a place called the new barracks, not far distant. The Right Hon. Charles Abbot, late speaker of the house of commons, who was a native of this neighbourhood, was, in 1817, elevated to the peerage by the title of Baron Colchester.

COLCHESTER, a township of Chittenden co., in the state of Vermont, U. S., 46 m. SW of Montpelier, indented on the W by Mallet's bay, an arm of Lake Champlain, and watered by Lamoille and Onion rivers. Pop. in 1840, 1,739. Partly in this t. is the flourishing village of Winooski.—Also a township of New London co., in the state of Connecticut, 24 m. SSE of Hartford, watered by Salmon river. It presents a hilly surface. Its soil is a fertile gravelly loam. Pop. 2,101.—Also a township of Delaware co., in the state of New York, 98 m. WSW of Albany, drained by Papacton branch of the Delaware river. It is generally hilly, but affords good pasturage. Pop. 1,567.

COLCURA, a village of Chili, in Araucania, 12 m. N of Arauco, finely situated on a high promontory which commands an extensive prospect of the country and the sea, with a distant view of the island Santa Maria.
COLD-ASHTON. See Ashton, Cold.
COLD-ASHBY. See Ashby, Cold.
COLD (CAPE). See FRIO.
COLD OR LITTLE HIGHAM, a parish of Northamptonshire, 4 m. NW of Towcester. Area 1,660 acres. Pop. 382.

COLDBROOK, a village of Worcester co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S., 61 m. W of Boston, on a brook of the same name.—Also a village of Russia township, Herkimer co., in the state of New York, 86 m. NW of Albany, on a stream of the same name. Pop. in 1840 about 300.
COLDEN, a township of Erie co., in the state of New York, 286 m. W of Albany. It is generally COL hilly, and is drained by Cazenove creek. Its soil is clayey and damp, but affords good pasturage.

COLDINGHAM, a parish on the coast of Berwickshire, skirting on East Lothian. Its superficies is hilly, consisting of several ranges of hills belonging to the Lammermuir chain. Area 57,600 acres. Pop. in 1801, 2,391; in 1841, 2,746. The hills are of inconsiderable elevation, the highest, Wardlaw bank, being only 640 ft. above the level of the sea; and they are cloven into ridges by intervening valleys of considerable extent, watered by the Eye, the Ale, their respective tributaries, and five minor streams. In the neighbourhood of St. Abb's head, the coast is rocky and dangerous, abounding in caves and fissures, once the retreat of smugglers, which are inaccessible by land, and can be approached by sea only at low water and in the calmest weather. About 2½ or 3 m. from St. Abb's head, on the S side of the v. of C., are some remains of the once celebrated priory of C.—The village of C. is 2 m. from Press inn, and 18 m. from Dunbar. Pop. in 1831, 850. It stands in a valley, about 1 m. distant from the sea.— The other villages are West Reston, about 21 m. from the SE extremity of the parish, with a pop. of 222; and Auchineraw, 13 m. to the SW, with a pop. of 161.
COLDITZ. See KOLDITZ.
COLDMEECE, a township in the p. and 31 m. Pop. 56.
COLD-NORTON, a parish of Essex, 5 m. S of Maldon. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. 264. — Also a township in the p. of Chebsey, Staffordshire. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 41.
COLD-OVERTON, a parish of Leicestershire, 7 m. SSE of Melton-Mowbray. Area 2,320 acres. Pop. 118.
COLDRED, a parish of Kent, 5 m. NNW of Dover. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 157.
COLDRIDGE. See COLERIDGE.

COLD-SPRING, a village of Phillipstown township, Putnam co., in the state of New York, U. S., 100 m. S of Albany, finely situated on the E side of Hudson river, 1 m. above West Point. Pop. in 1840, 1,250.—Also a township of Cattaraugus co., in the same state, watered by Alleghany river and its tributary Cold Spring creek, and on the line of the New York and Erie railroad. Pop. 673.—Also a village of Lower township, Cape May co., in the state of New Jersey, 105 m. S of Trenton.

COLD-SPRING HARBOUR, a village of Oyster Bay and Huntingdon townships, in Queen's and Suffolk co., in the state of New York, U.S., 186 m. SE of Albany, on both sides of Cold Spring harbour. Pop. in 1840, about 500. It has some whaling ships, and numerous coasting vessels.

COLDSTREAM, a parish in the co. of Berwick, bounded by the Tweed, which separates it from England, on the S. The elevation of Coldstream-bridge over the Tweed is 61 ft. above Berwick pier. The Leet flows for a part of its course through the parish. Pop. in 1801, 2,269; in 1831, 2,897; in 1841, 2,857. -The town of C. lies upon the N bank of the Tweed, having the Leet flowing past it on its W quarter. It is <math>9\frac{1}{4}</math> m. E of Kelso; <math>10\frac{3}{4}</math> m. SW of Dunse; and 143 m. W of Berwick. The road from Edinburgh to Wooler, by Cornhill, crosses the Tweed at Coldstream by a bridge of 5 arches. Cornhill is 1 m., Wooler 15 m., and Newcastle-on-Tyne 61 m. distant from Coldstream. The town formerly derived consequence from a ford over the Tweed, the first of any importance which occurs in following the stream upwards from Berwick. It was last used by a Scottish army, as an entrance into England, in 1640. On the last Thursday of every month a cattle-market ~ is held here, which is chiefly resorted to by dealers from the north of England. Pop. in 1841, 1,913.
COLD-STREAM, a village of Hampshire co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 21 m. E of Romney, on the W bank of Great Cacapon river.
COLD-WALTHAM, a parish of Sussex, 21 N of Bury, bounded on the W by the Arun. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. 460.
COLD-WATER, a township of Branch co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., 110 m. WSW of Detroit, intersected by a river of the same name and its affluents. Pop. in 1840, 1,123.

COLD-WATER LAKE, a small lake in Upper Canada, in N lat. 48° 59', W long. 90° 14' [Delafield, lying on the dividing range betwixt the basin of Lake Superior and the waters flowing to Hudson's bay. From its source, which is 8 ft. above the level of the lake, there is a fall of 505 ft. to Lake Superior in a direct distance of 50 m. It communicates by Dog river with Dog lake, which discharges itself into Lake Superior by the Dog or Kaministaguia.
COLE. See PITCOMBE.

COLE, a county of the state of Missouri, U.S., comprising an area of 650 sq. m., bounded on the N by the S bank of the Missouri, and on the SW by the Osage river, and watered by Moreau creek. Pop. in 1830, 3,023: in 1840, 9,286. Its cap. is Jefferson city.—Also a township of Benton co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. in 1840, 817.
COLEBATCH, a township in the p. of Bishop's Castle, Salop. Pop. 104.
COLEBATH, a township of Clarke co., in the state of Arkansas, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 169.
COLEBROOK, a parish of Devon, 4 m. W of Crediton, N of the Yew. Area 4,200 acres. Pop. 878.

COLEBROOK-DALE, a hamlet in the p. of Broseley, co. of Salop, 2 m. N of Broseley, on the N bank of the Severn. It is celebrated for its iron bridge, erected in 1779 over the Severn, about 1 m. from Madeley-Market, and consisting of an arch 40 feet high, and more than 100 feet span. The first idea of constructing railroads is supposed to have been entertained between the years 1620 and 1650, when they were roughly constructed of wood; upwards of 100 years elapsed before a decided improvement was introduced, originating at the C. iron-works, where iron plates were first put on wooden rails in 1787. C. gives name to a winding glen through which the Severn flows, between two hills; the tout ensemble of which, with the forges, engines, furnaces, and vast machinery of the iron works, constitutes a spectacle of the most interesting description. Geologists have also given the name of C. to a rich coal and iron district, which has long been known to them as a point of great interest. It includes this celebrated defile, and extends E to Lilleshall and Bridgenorth; NW to Wellington, and thence by the Wrekin; SE to the road from Much Wenlock to Bridgenorth; while on the W the boundary is broken by the gorge of the Severn, but is formed in part by the elevated ridges of Benthal and Wenlock. The area thus circumscribed consists of a platform raised about 400 ft. above the Severn at Madeley, about 1 m. from C., or 500 ft. above the level of the sea. Mr. Prestwich, F. G. S., gives the following account of the number of beds of coal at each of the principal works, and the aggregate thickness:- Yards. Feet. Inches. No. of beds. Hadley. 2
0
2
2 6 ... 11
0 ... 16
0 ... 17 Langley, . ... COL At these points the coal-measures are fully developed, and consequently the difference in the number and thickness of the strata does not arise from a diminution of the system, but in some cases may be accounted for by the minor beds not having been deemed worthy of notice. Next in importance to the beds of coal are the layers of argillaceous carbonate of iron. This valuable ore generally occurs in flattened nodules, constituting regular seams, which are distinguished by the names of pennystone, the chance stone, the ball stone, the ragged robins, &c. Some of these layers extend throughout the field, but others are of local occurrence, and the aggregate number in a pit varies from two to seven. They are generally imbedded in shale, but occasionally in sandstone. In some parts of the district, and situated near to the top of the series, is a bed of fresh water limestone. - A petroleum or tar spring, for which C. has been long celebrated, issues from a thick bed of sandstone in the upper part of the coal measures; it yielded formerly more than a hogshead a-day, but produces now only a few gallons a-week. Another spring has been discovered, and petroleum is frequently found to some extent in working the coal. The fossils contained in the coal-measures are of great interest, as they occur in considerable abundance, and consist of a curious association of remains, including not only those of terrestrial plants and fresh water shells, but also those of marine testacea and other animals. Mr. Prestwich conceives that these phenomena may be explained by supposing that the coal-measures were originally accumulated in an estuary occasionally subjected to considerable freshes from a large river.
COLEBROOKDALE, a township of Berks co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 73 m. E of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,124.
COLEBURY, a tything in the p. of Eling, Hants. Pop. 341.
COLEBY, a parish of Lincolnshire, 8 m. S of Lincoln, E of the Brant. Area 2,600 acres. Pop. 427. —Also a hamlet partly in the parishes of Burton-on- Stether and West Halton. Pop. 68.
COLE CREEK, a township of Montgomery co., in the state of Indiana, U.S. Pop. in 1840, 1,589.
COLEDALE, or Portingscale, a township in the p. of Crosthwaite, Cumberland, 3 m. W of Keswick, and W of the Derwent water. Pop. 262. See BRAITHWAITE.
COLEFORD, a chapelry in the p. of Newland, Gloucestershire, 7½ m. WNW of Blakeney, bounded on the W by the Wye. Pop. 2,208.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Kilmersdon, Somerset. Pop. 825.
COLEMERE, a township in the p. of Ellesmere, Salop. Pop. 192.
COLEMORE, or Colmere, a parish of Hants, 51 m. SSW of Alton. Area 1,270 acres. Pop. 144.
COLE-ORTON, a parish of Leicestershire, 2 m. E of Ashby-de-la-Zouch, and near the Leicester railway. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 601.

COLERAIN, a township of Franklin co., in the state of Mississippi, U. S., 101 m. NW of Boston, watered by a branch of Deerfield river, and affording excellent pasturage. Pop. in 1840, 1,971.—Also a township of Ross co., in the state of Ohio. Pop. 1,281.—Also a township of Lancaster co., in the state of Pennsylvania, 69 m. SE of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,453.—Also a township of Bedford co., in the same state. Pop. 5,190.—Also a township of Hamilton co., in the state of Ohio, 10 m. N of Cincinnati,

COLERAINE, a parish in the Liberties of Cole- i raine, co. Londonderry. It lies along the r. bank of the Bann, and contains the larger section of the town of Coleraine. Area 4,846 acres. Pop. in 1831, 5,668; in 1841, 5,857. Houses 1,037.

COLERAINE, a market and post-town, a seaport, a borough, and the second town in importance N or NW of Belfast, stands on the river Bann, partly in the p. of Killowen, but chiefly in that of Coleraine, co. Londonderry. C. Proper, including all the original and old town, stands on the E bank of the river, and in the p. of Coleraine; and the conjoint suburb of Killowen and Waterside, which usually shares the town's name, and is included in both the old and the new borough boundaries, stands on the W bank of the river, in the p. of Killowen. The town is situated 4 m. S of Portrush, 46 m. NNW of Belfast, and 1331 m. or 1191 m. by different routes N by W of Dublin. After many years of improvement and prosperity, C. is not, as to edifices, streetalignment, or general effect, a good-looking town, though comparatively free from the poverty and meanness which characterize so many towns and villages in the central and southern divisions of the kingdom. The whole town, excepting small parts of the outskirts, is compressed within a square superficies of about 520 yards each way. The bridge which connects the two sections of the town is of a structure well-suited to its site upon a large volume and rapid current of water: its piers are of stone, and its flooring, span-pieces, and ceiling are of wood.—The linen manufacture is greatly the most important department of productive industry; but has been subject to considerable fluctuations. The fabric made, well-known in trade as 'Coleraines,' is woven principally in private houses, and in the cottages of the surrounding peasantry; it is bleached in considerable quantity in the neighbourhood for the London market; and is now principally exported direct from Coleraine and Portrush. The only other noticeable manufactures are tanning and soap-boiling. The exports from Coleraine and Portrush, in 1835, amounted in estimated value to £105,685; and the principal items were corn, meal, and flour, £40,954,—provisions, chiefly bacon and butter, £42,527,—linen, £5,120,— flax and tow, £4,950,—and wool, £1,100. The export and import trade is greatly marred by the existence of the bar at the mouth of the Bann, and by the want of navigable communication with Lough Neagh; yet the former disadvantage is remedied partly by the comparatively near vicinity of Portrush, and partly by the navigable capacity of the Bann to Coleraine for vessels of 200 tons burden, and the latter disadvantage may probably be remedied at an early date by the cutting of a canal, or compensated by the construction of a railway. Regularly plying steam-vessels now connect Coleraine by Portrush with Fleetwood, with Londonderry, with Liverpool, and with the Clyde; and a project was entertained, so far back as 14 or 15 years ago, to connect the town by a railway to Armagh with the proposed railway ramifications throughout the kingdom. The borough, previous to the legislative union, sent two members to the Irish parliament; it now sends one member to the parliament of the United Kingdom. Constituency, in 1841, 368.—Area of the p. of Coleraine section of the town, 325 acres; of the Killowen section, 222 acres. Pop. of the whole, in 1831, 5,752; in 1841, 6,255. Houses 1,132. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 113; in manufactures and trade, 525; in other pursuits, 214.—The name Coleraine is possibly a corruption of Cuil-rathen, which signifies 'the corner of ferns,' and may allude to the profuse growth of ferns in the sandy warrens of the Bann toward the sea; or, with greater probability, it is | which have their sources in the Bambouk territory. COL derived from Cuil-rath-ean, 'the fort on the corner or bend of the waters.' In 1613, a number of London merchants, who were incorporated by charter under the designation of "the Governor and Assistants of the New Plantation of Ulster," received a grant of C. and a great part of the county of Londonderry; and their successors, usually called the Irish or the Hon. Irish Society, still hold the property, but only as superiors; for the town is let on leases under them, and an extensive adjacent estate is held on perpetuity from them by the marquis of Waterford. -Coleraine gives the title of Baron to the noble family of Hanger.
COLERAIN-FORGE, a village of Huntingdon co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 106 m. W of Harrisburg, on Spruce creek.
COLERIDGE, or Coldridge, a parish of Devon, 4½ m. S of Chumleigh, W of the Taw. Area 2,190 acres. Pop. 677.
COLERNE, a parish of Wilts, 6½ m. WSW of Chippenham, near the Great Western railway. Area 3,620 acres. Pop. 1,209.

COLERUN, a river of Hindostan, in the prov. of the Carnatic, forming the largest and most northerly of the branches of the Cavery, from which it separates near Trichinopoly, below the island of Seringham. After a course of about 80 m., it falls into the bay of Bengal, at Porto-Novo. See CAVERY.

COLES, a county of the state of Illinois, U. S., comprising an area of 1,248 sq. m., drained by Kaskasia and Embarrass, and the head-streams of Little Wabash rivers. It has an undulating surface, with extensive prairies, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 9,616. Its capital is Charleston.
COLES POINT, a promontory on the coast of Bolivia, in the intendancy of Arequipa, in S lat. 17° 42', W long. 71° 26' 15".—Beechey.

COLESBERG, a district in the E province of the Cape of Good Hope, bounded by the New Gariep on the N. Area 11,654 sq. m. Pop. in 1838, 2,100; in 1845, 8,828. It is an elevated district, well-adapted for grazing.—Its cap., of the same name, is in S lat. 30° 40′, E long. 25° 30′. Pop. 500.
COLESBORNE, or COLDSBORNE, a parish of Gloucestershire, 8 m. NNW of Circucester. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 256.

COLESHILL, a parish, partly in Berks and Wilts, chiefly in the former, 33 m. WSW of Great Farringdon, bounded on the W by the Cole. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. 386.—Also a hamlet in the p. and <math>1\frac{3}{4}</math> m. SSW of Amersham, Hertfordshire. Area 2,810 acres. Pop. 547.—Also a parish of Warwickshire, 17 m. NNW of Warwick, on the S bank of the Cole. Area 6,200 acres. Pop. 2,172.
COLESHILL-FAWR, a township in the p. of Holywell, Flint. Pop. 643.
COLESHY, a small maritime town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Travancore, 19 m. NW of Comorin. The harbour is small, but safe and well sheltered.

COLESSEAH, a village in the island of Socotra, on the NW coast, comprising about 50 Arab families, who have a few fishing-boats, and supply vessels with fowls and sheep. Dragon's blood, aloes, and ghee, are shipped here in small quantities by the buggalows which occasionally put in here.

COLESVILLE, a township of Broome co., in the state of New York, 124 m. WSW of Albany, intersected by the Susquehanna river, the banks of which are formed of rich alluvial soil. The other parts of the township are hilly. Pop. in 1840, 2,528.
COLETO, a river of Texas, an affluent of the Guadalupe, which it joins on the r. bank after a SE course of 65 m.

COLEZ, a name borne by two rivers of W Africa, The Western C., the Rio d'Oro of the Portuguese, joins the Falemme at Naye-Mow, after a course of about 90 m. The Eastern C., or Gayamon C., enters the Senegal at Bukayakulu.

COLGONG, or KAHALGANG, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 110 m. ENE of Bahar, district and 20 m. E of Boglipur, on the r. bank of the Goga, 5 m. S of its confluence with the Ganges, and enclosed by hills, which form a fine amphitheatre, the summit of which commands one of the finest prospects in the entire course of the Ganges.
COLIBRE (MONTE), the highest hill on the largest of the Columbretes islets, near the coast of Valencia in Spain. The name is given by D'Anville to the whole group of the Columbretes.

COLICO, a village and small port of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 27 m. NE of Como, on the l. bank of the lake of that name, 3 m. SSW of the confluence of the Adda, and on the road from Lecco to Sondrio.

COLIGNY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Ain, arrond. of Bourgen-Bresse. The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,815; in 1841, 9,878. The town, which is 15 m. NNE of Bourg-en-Bresse, rises amphitheatrally on the acclivity of a mountain; and on an adjacent hill are the remains of a castle, which belonged to the family of Chatillon. Pop. in 1846, 1,697. It has a considerable trade in corn, wine, hemp, cattle, horses, and fowls. Fairs are held twice a-year.
COLIHAUT, a town on the W coast of the island of Dominica, in the p. of St. Peter, at the mouth of a river of the same name, 14 m. NNW of Roseau.

COLIMA, a district of Mexico, in the state of Xalisco, comprising the low country surrounding the great Volcano-de-Colima. Its chief productions are cotton and salt. Its pop. is estimated at 40,000.— Its cap., of the same name, is a small town situated 2 leagues S of the Volcano. Beechey, by a measurement from a base of 48 m., estimates the alt. of the Volcano-de-C. at 12,003 ft. above sea-level. It is in N lat. 19° 25′ 24", long. 1° 41′ 42" E of San Blas. It is the most western of the volcanoes of Mexico. It is never covered with snow except when it falls on the neighbouring mountains with a N wind.
COLINA, a village of Chili, in the prov. of Aconcagua, on the road from Chacabuco to Santiago-de- Chili, 10 leagues from the former, and 13 from the latter town. It is celebrated for its baths.

COLINDA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, and district of Tiperah, near the Rumatcally, 28 m. E of Luckipur, 75 m. SE of Dacca. It possesses extensive manufactories of coarse cotton fabrics. The surrounding country is flat and swampy.
